Plato was a [[classical greek]] philosopher. He was the teacher of [[Aristotle]] and the student of [[socrates]].

==Vervarke Notes==
He came up with the idea of [[Plato's Lion]]. Which I confused as being "Aristotle's Lion" due to the similarly named [[wikipedia:Aristotle's Lantern|Aristotle's Lantern]].
==Class notes==
Missed first lecture but it was mostly syllabus stuff

== Background ==
Greece did not have a democracy. We are starting at the beginning of the 5th century

They used sortition

Only exception to sortition was military generals

=== Trial system ===
people volunteered to be jurors. Tended to be older people. They would be chosen by lot. 50 people so nobody could bribe

=== Theater system ===
People would ridicule decisions of the society in the theater. 

=== Impiety ===
If you were a blacksmith and did a bad job you would be charged with impiety. It was about the idea you had bad intentions (didn't worship Hephaestus)

==[[Wikipedia:Ion (dialogue)|Ion (dialogue)]]==
Someone said they were knowledgeable about Homer but not Hesiod. 

He said it might be the case that poets were like magnets

=== Three Kinds of knowledge mentioned in the lecture ===

* [[Phenomenal knowing|Experiential]]
* [[Procedural knowing|Practical]]
* [[Propositional knowing|Propositional]]
The idea of the knowledge processing has parallels in John Locke. 

==Justification: Internalism vs Externalism==
Internalism means you need to know you know

Externalism means you just need a reliable mechanism

== 1-16 lesser hippias ==
Truth vs lying

ἀληθής vs ψευδής

Plato argues being a deliberate liar is better than an unwilling or unintentional liar

According to Hippias Achilles  was the best and bravest of the heores of troy, and truthful and simpl

Odysseus was a great leader in part because he was a liar

Plato talked a lot about equivocations in the early work. We are going through his work in chronological order in this class

== Words in it ==
Kalos

Good/Moral/Skillfu/Honorable/Noble/Beautful/Handsome

Arete

Excellence/Virtue/Knowledge/Fulfillment/Living up to one's potential

Eudaimonia

Good-spirit blessed by the gods, etc

Eusebeia

Piety/Godliness/Duty/Loyalty

=== Kalos vs Arete ===
Arete is outcomes and Kalos is potential

== 1-18 Laches/Courage ==
Plato's Laches is a dialogue analyzing courage


Laches and Nicias are two of Athens' ten generals

Asked by two fathers if young 


Nicias says young men should train in armor

Laches says young men should not


Laches says to be brave is to stand and fight

Socrates says it only applies to infantry (and only when not ordered to retreat)


Nicias says Bravery is knowledge of the fearful and the hopeful

== Lysis dialogue ==


Hippothales is in love with Lysis (gay)

Socrates asks 


Lysis was not allowed to ride a chariot yet. 

Parental love before he has reached mastery

Lysis says friendship is the most important thing to him  

Iterates over   

Trying to define friendship 

# Friendship between people who are similar, interpreted by Socrates as friendship between good men.
## Cannot work because good people do not need friends
# Friendship between men who are dissimilar.
## Cannot work because a good and bad person cannot be friends
# Friendship between men who are neither good nor bad and good men.
## Irvine says people trying to improve
## Cannot work since it turns into a friendship between two good people
# Gradually emerging: friendship between those who are relatives (οἰκεῖοι "not kindred") by the nature of their souls.
## unclear 

=== Friendships between similar people ===
Plato says that it might be people who are good, because bad people cannot be good friends, so this measn only between good people

=== Aristotle's three kinds of friendship ===

# Friendship based on utility
## helping each other
## often between the young
# Friendship based on pleasure
## pleasures of interaction
## often between the young
# Friendship based on goodness
## admire each other's goodness
## both strive to help the other improve
## often translated as brotherly love

[[Category:John Vervaeke]]
[[Category:Git synced pages]]
[[sn:Plato]][[Category:Philosophers]]
[[Category:College]]
[[sn:Plato]][[sn:Plato]]==Gorgias 20:32, 30 January 2024 (UTC)==
This is Gorgias dialogue

Disagreement over whether oratory is an art (techne) or a knack (empirike)

Socrates considered character the most important thing. 

This one is a really authentic one from Socrates rather than Plato's views

=== Arguments for rhetoric ===

* Prepares young people to speak in the Assembly and the Courts

=== Dramatis personae ===
# Gorgias
## a famous foreign teacher of oratory
## in athens because the money was there
## Promises to show young men and women how to win arguments
## Teaches life skills and values designed to lead to arete
# Polus
## a young man (name means young horse "colt")
## Wants to learn rhetoric so he can become powerful
# Callicles
## An older successful influential Athenian citizen
## A genuine matchup with Socrates. About on par with Socrates
## Argues that the more able people should have greater political power
### His idea was more that people were born with this ability

=== Gorgias ===
Socrates asks Gorgias the name of his profession

He says he is an orator and a teacher of oratory

Socrates asks about the goal of oratory. 

Gorgias says it is the craft of influencing people by speech


Socrates questions him on this saying that you need to know abotu different things

Gorgias says it is about the greatest of human concerns

=== Polus ===
Polus asks Socrates what he thinks Oratory is


Socrates says oratory is a knack (empirike) for producing gratification and pleasure through flattery


A knack wouldn't have a content of piety, but an art (techne) would have a content of piety


Socrates says

* Just as there are four true crafts that care for the healthy of the body and soul, there are four types of persuasive flattery
** Care for body
*** Gymnastics
*** Medicine
** Care for soul
*** Legislation
*** Justice
* Types of flattery
** Flattery for the body
*** Cosmetics pretends to be gymnastics
*** Pastry baking pretends to be medicine
** Flattery for the soul
*** Sophistry pretends to be legislation
*** Oratory pretends to be like justice


Polus says Oratory may be a knack, but even if it is one it is still a useful knack. 



Socrates says that it is better to be 



Russell vs Popper debate

Russel argued evil came from immoral impulses

Popper argued 

=== Callicles point ===
Warning Socrates he will be put on trial and with his disdain for rhetoric he is not able to defend himself


Nature reveals that it's a good thing for the better man to rule


Socrates asks


Shouldn't his slaves take over in this situation?

== February 6 ==
Plato may have been more womens rights supportive due to thinking in abstract more


Thucydides was an Athenian historian and general.

== Knowledge ==


phronesis is practical knowledge

== Protagoras ==


A student of Democritus

Sometimes called the first sophist

About 20 years older than Socrates,


Protagoras does not believe in objective truths. This is socrates' view but unsure whether he believed that himself

On the way to Callias' house Socrates and Hippocrates are walking

Socrates warns Hippocrates about sophists

Then they get to the area

Protagoras and

== Theatetus ==
Theatetus is a really notable mathematician potentially


Or died young in war

== The Republic ==
Called Justice in medieval texts


Laws is a later book written 30 years later. 

=== Overview of The Republic ===
Main topics

* the nature of '''justice'''
* the relation between '''justice''' and '''eudaemonia'''
* The form of an '''ideal state'''
* The '''importance of education'''
* the distinction between '''appearance''' and '''reality'''
* the theory of the '''forms'''
* Immortality of the '''mind or soul (psyche)'''
* The nature of '''knowledge''' (and whether the truth is '''relative''')

=== Is the republic historically accurate to Socrates ===
Wide consensus is he is not


In Crito he argues for democracy, in the Republic he argues against it

=== What is Justice ===

* Justice is not a matter of simpy returning what is owed since you do not give back a weapon to someone who has gone insane
* Not just a matter of helping friends and harming enemies

According to Thrasymachus Socrates' ideas are a bunch of hot air



Theories of law

# Justice is based on past custom
# Justice is based on the supernatural
# Justice is based on the way the world works (physis)

=== Glaucon's ring of gyges ===
It is good to steal if it effectively achives your interest

=== Ideal state ===
Plato tries to move onto establishing what is justice from what a just city would be, therefore what a just individual would be


Is the republic (professor talking)

# a blueprint for the perfect city
# a warning for what can do wrong with utopian planning
# a prompt


Plato proposed specialization . Appears to contradict the earlier narratives which were criticizing specialization.

People are distributed differently with different ideas, seems to contradict Protagoras narrative. 

=== Tripartite nature of the soul ===

# Man
# [[Plato's Lion|Lion]]
# Beast


He takes that this division of the spirit ought to lead to a division of the classes of the citizens

# Overseers
# Auxiliaries
# Craftsmen


Overseers come from the older more experienced Auxiliaries


Guardian training

# basic education until age 18
# two years military training
# A decade of training in mathematics
# five years of training in philosophy and dialectic
# fifteen years as leaders
# At 50 they will be fully aware of the form of good

Is it more like people start out as auxiliaries and specialize into craftsmen or overseers? Where would career soldiers who did not have aptitude for philosophy fit?

=== Three waves of attack ===
Three criticisms of the idea


Why should

# Men and women receive the same education. 
## Unwated pregnancies could happen
## men and women have different natures
## Wrestle together naked? Some people say this is ironic
# Why should children be held in common away from their biological parents
## Eugenics
# The ideal city and its philosopher kind be anything more than wishful thinking

== The Laws ==
not taught nearly as much as the Republic

People say it is dull


Great kingdoms fall due to a "disease of kings" where luxury corrupts them

=== Checks against corruption ===
Philosopher kings cannot be trusted to govern

# Absolute power leads to corruption


Mixed government

Plato says that there are two different kinds of government

* Monarchy (Persia)
** Fals because too much power leads to corruption
* Democracy (Athens)
** Fails because too much freedom leads to a lack of respect for authority
*** Lack of respect for the objective good


His idea of a mixed government was kind of separation of powers, but not really developed fully


He says there are two central ideas

* good judgment
** a sense of proportion, so we don't try to fit excessively large sails to small ships
* rule of law
** modified appointment procedures for officials
** modified court system

== Aristotle's politics ==
Says that the Republic is kind of crazy but the laws is better

== Symposium ==
Plato's symposium, Irvine says it comes before the Apology. It is about gay love and Irvine argues defense of the city.


A bunch of guys getting drunk together is the setting of the dialogue


Eros is the oldest god

=== Phaedrus ===
says that people are pushed by shame and sacrifice related to those they love

=== Pausanias ===
Says there are two kinds of love

* Common love
** men and women
** men and boys
* Heavenly love
** older men and younger men (never boys)
Place legal obstacles in place to put heavenly love above common love

Names different customs

* In Boeootia gay relationships are accepted
* In Persia gay relationships are prohibited
* In athens same sex relationships are permissed only when based on heavenly love

=== Eryximachus ===
Love exists in plants and animals


Probably referred to pollination. People were quite close to the natural world and this can be foreign to modern audiences.



Our challenge is to learn to distinguish Healthy Love from Unhealthy love

* In healthy love it is honorable to 

=== Aristophanes ===
A playwright "The father of comedy"

Questions the existing of gods

Tells the fantastic story aout how every human was at one time two people conjoined together

=== Agathon ===
????

=== Socrates ===
Socrates says he had a teacher named Diotima who was a woman who taught him everything about love

Says Eros is partially god partially mortal. Mediates between people and the objects of their desites

=== Alcibiades ===
Rich and handsome and famous and drunk

He is drunker than everyone else and proposes a speech to Socrates rather than Eros

Compares Socrates to a statue of Silenus (Matrioshka doll like idols with golden statues on the inside)


Socrates was the only person to make him feel shame


Unrequited love of socrates

== Euthyphro ==
God as a moral rule maker or a moral expert
[[Category:Pages]]
